front: Composer: German; composed his 9th Symphony while completely deaf; famous for tearing up Symphony No. 3's dedication page to Napoleon
Composer: wrote An die ferne Geliebte (often called the first song cycle), setting six poems by Alois Isidor Jetteles; quoted in Schumann's Fantasy in C
Composer: originally dedicated the Kreutzer Sonata to George Bridgetower (who premiered it); re-dedicated it to Rodolphe Kreutzer after Bridgetower insulted a friend
Composer: the Beethoven Frieze by Gustav Klimt covers three walls of the Vienna Secession Building; its central section features the giant Typhoeus and the Gorgons
Symphony: E-flat major; bears a retracted dedication to Napoleon; second movement is an unusually long funeral march; finale is a set of variations on a theme from The Creatures of Prometheus
Symphony: opens with two isolated E-flat forte chords; violins enter in bar 7 with syncopated G's above a sustained C-sharp resolving to D
Symphony: opens with 'G, G, G, long E-flat' — the 'fate knocks at the door' motif; first oboe plays an unexpected adagio cadenza in the recapitulation
Symphony: C-minor scherzo (pizzicato) quietly reprises in the middle of the C-major finale; E. M. Forster described this as a return of 'the goblins'
Symphony: 'Pastoral'; five programmatic movements; bird calls from flute, oboe, and clarinets in 'Scene by the brook'; storm with low tremolo gives way to a Thanksgiving hymn
Symphony: Wagner called it 'the apotheosis of dance'; second movement (Allegretto, but slowest) has a long-short-short-long-long ostinato in A minor
Symphony: 'Choral'; finale opens with a 'terror fanfare,' then cello/bass recitative rejects previous themes; Turkish March section imitates janissary bands with piccolo, triangle, and tenor
Sonata: C-sharp minor; first movement has constant triplet arpeggios; instruction to hold pedal down constantly; dedicated to student Julie Guicciardi; nicknamed by Ludwig Rellstab
Sonata: C minor; slow Grave introduction returns in the development; Adagio cantabile in A-flat inspired Elgar's 'Nimrod'
Sonata: F minor; begins with arpeggios on the tonic chord and the Neapolitan (G-flat major) chord
Sonata: B-flat major; named for the German word for the piano; ends with a three-voice Allegro risoluto fugue
Concerto: Violin Concerto in D major; opens with five timpani taps; dedicated to Franz Clement ('with clemency, for Clement'); called 'greatest, most uncompromising' by Joseph Joachim
Concerto: 'Emperor' (No. 5, E-flat major); opens with a tutti E-flat chord followed by ascending arpeggios from the soloist; finale is attacca from the second movement
Work: originally the finale of String Quartet No. 13 (B-flat major, Op. 130); published independently; called 'contemporary forever' by Stravinsky
String Quartet: No. 16, F major; final movement 'The Difficult Decision' inscribed with 'Must it be? It must be!'
